result:
# The Peculiar Comfort of Unfinished Things

I have been thinking lately about the drawer in my kitchen — the one that doesn't quite close all the way. Every apartment I've ever imagined having, every house described to me, seems to have one of these drawers. A drawer that sticks. A drawer that requires a specific, learned gesture: a slight lift, a push to the left, a firm press at exactly the right corner. Visitors don't know how to close it. They try once, fail, and leave it hanging open at an awkward angle, half-embarrassed, as though they've failed some basic competency test.

And yet the person who lives there doesn't even think about it anymore. The gesture is entirely automatic. The drawer is never actually open, not to them.

I find this strangely moving.

There's a whole category of things like this — imperfect systems that become so well-understood by those who inhabit them that the imperfection essentially disappears. Old cars with quirks. Doors that only unlock if you push inward while turning the key. Staircases where the third step creaks and everyone who lives there has simply learned to step over it, so that only guests make noise going up. The house communicates with its long-term residents in a private language. It shuts up for everyone else.

I think about this in relation to expertise generally. Most genuine expertise looks, from the outside, like either magic or effortlessness. The experienced surgeon knows, in some way that cannot be fully articulated, that something is slightly off before the instruments confirm it. The jazz musician hears the chord about to go wrong and adjusts before the wrong note lands. The kindergarten teacher reads a room of twenty-five five-year-olds the way a sailor reads weather — not item by item but atmospherically, as a total condition that her body has learned to respond to before her mind has finished processing. The sticky drawer, handled without thought.

What interests me is the moment before that fluency arrives. The learning period. When the staircase is just a staircase and you hit that creaking step every single time, and you feel slightly guilty, and you're aware of yourself in a way that feels unpleasant. Self-consciousness is often described as a social problem — embarrassment, shyness, the fear of being watched — but I think it's more fundamentally an epistemic condition. It's the state of not yet knowing something well enough to do it without watching yourself do it. And that state is genuinely uncomfortable in a way that's almost physical, a friction, a drag.

Learning anything is a long sequence of self-consciousness gradually dissolving. You are aware of your hands on the steering wheel, then one day you're not. You think about where to put your fingers on the guitar neck, then one day you're thinking about something else entirely and your fingers are just there, already in position. The self retreats from the activity. The activity becomes, as we say, second nature — which is a remarkable phrase, actually, suggesting that we grow additional natures as we go, layers of instinct that are really just old learning compacted down below the threshold of attention.

I wonder sometimes what's lost in that compaction. When the sticking drawer becomes automatic, you stop noticing the drawer at all. Which is efficient. But the drawer is also, in some sense, a small interesting thing in the world — an object with a specific material history, a problem someone once tried to fix and couldn't, a minor texture in the ongoing experience of being alive in a particular place. Fluency can produce a kind of blindness. The expert stops seeing what beginners see.

This might be part of why travel is so reliably interesting. In an unfamiliar place, everything is sticky again. Every door is a puzzle. You don't know which light switch controls which light, and so you notice the lights. You don't know the rhythm of the city streets, so you notice the streets. A kind of enforced beginner's mind. You are returned to self-consciousness, which is uncomfortable, but the dividend is that the world becomes textured again, present again, full of drawer-like resistance that demands your attention.

Some artists try to manufacture this deliberately — to stay in the beginner's posture, to keep the world strange. Certain poets won't use a word that has become too smooth, too automatic in the reader's mouth. They reach for the less traveled word, the slightly wrong word, the word that makes you slow down, that makes you feel the staircase step again. This technique has a Russian formalist name — defamiliarization — which sounds more clinical than the experience deserves. What it really describes is the art of putting the stickiness back in.

The trouble is that you can't fully manufacture it. Real strangeness is unrepeatable. You can return to the same foreign city, and by the third visit you know which way to turn out of the train station, and some of the magic has migrated. The drawer has started to learn your hand.

Maybe this is fine. Maybe the constant cycling — understanding earned, strangeness lost, new strangeness sought — is just what a life is. We metabolize our experiences into fluency and then go looking for new friction. Children do this involuntarily, because everything is new. Adults have to be more deliberate. They have to choose to put themselves in rooms where they don't know how anything works.

I don't have a clean conclusion here. I'm not sure what to do with any of this except notice it — which might be the point. The noticing itself is a small resistance to the compacting force of habit. To sit and think about the sticky drawer instead of just closing it without thought. To give the imperfect thing a moment of actual attention before it vanishes into the smooth machinery of the known.

The drawer that doesn't quite close. The step that always creaks. The key that needs the particular touch.

These are the textures of being somewhere specific, knowing somewhere specifically, and they're worth, I think, at least one slow look before they disappear.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
